title: Weekly synthesis: CIA Triad, Risk, Threat
objective: Lock in the week's material, surface weak spots, and tidy your notes.
checkpoint: Can define cybersecurity in business terms, not just technical terms.
plain_language_intro: Today reviews the week's main concepts as one connected model.
mini_lecture_markdown:
This is the review day for Google Course 1: Foundations of Cybersecurity. The goal is to connect the week's main ideas into one working model rather than introduce a new topic. The core terms are CIA Triad, Risk, Threat, Vulnerability, Control, and Security Framework.

Across the week, the key mechanisms were: CIA Triad: evaluates whether data stays secret, unaltered, and reachable, then uses those goals to choose and review controls Risk: combines the chance of a harmful event with the likely impact so work can be ranked and resourced Threat: becomes actionable when a capable actor or condition meets an exposed weakness and a path to the target Vulnerability: appears when a flaw or misconfiguration leaves a reachable gap that an attacker or failure condition can abuse The value of the review is in seeing how these pieces connect across setup, operation, failure, and troubleshooting.

For Google Cybersecurity Professional Certificate, this block is most useful when you can separate similar concepts, place them at the right layer, and connect them to symptoms or evidence. That usually means following a chain from cause to behavior to visible result.

At this point, the week should read as one sequence rather than separate definitions. Each concept should connect to a role, a mechanism, and a likely scenario where it matters.

One useful review lens is to track how the week's concepts relate to symptoms, evidence, and next-step decisions in a short scenario.

The main point of the review is to keep the concepts connected rather than treat them as isolated terms.
exam_relevance_markdown: Expect this material to show up as scenario-style reasoning rather than raw recall. Typical tasks include distinguishing related concepts, choosing the right component or control, identifying the most likely cause from symptoms, and explaining why one workflow step belongs before another. If you can reconstruct the week without the notes open, you are closer to the level these exams reward.
key_distinctions: CIA Triad vs Risk — CIA Triad: Security model built around confidentiality, integrity, and availability. Risk: Possibility that a threat will exploit a weakness and cause harm to an asset or process.

Risk vs Vulnerability — Risk: Possibility that a threat will exploit a weakness and cause harm to an asset or process. Vulnerability: Weakness in design, implementation, configuration, or process that can be exploited.

Threat vs Vulnerability — Threat: Potential source of harm that could exploit a weakness or disrupt normal operations. Vulnerability: Weakness in design, implementation, configuration, or process that can be exploited.

Vulnerability vs Threat — Vulnerability: Weakness in design, implementation, configuration, or process that can be exploited. Threat: Potential source of harm that could exploit a weakness or disrupt normal operations.
Control vs Compensating Control — Control: Safeguard that prevents, detects, or corrects a security problem. Compensating Control: Alternative safeguard used when a primary control is not feasible.
mental_model_markdown: Use a pipeline model for this day: input arrives, a component or service processes it, state changes occur, and an output or symptom becomes visible to the user, administrator, or analyst. CIA Triad: evaluates whether data stays secret, unaltered, and reachable, then uses those goals to choose and review controls Risk: combines the chance of a harmful event with the likely impact so work can be ranked and resourced Threat: becomes actionable when a capable actor or condition meets an exposed weakness and a path to the target Vulnerability: appears when a flaw or misconfiguration leaves a reachable gap that an attacker or failure condition can abuse That sequence is the main mental model for the topic.
self_check_prompts: Explain the difference between CIA Triad and Risk without using the words "better" or "faster" as your whole answer.
Describe what CIA Triad does, what input it works on, and what evidence would show that it is functioning correctly.
Walk through today's objective in sequence and say which step would fail first if the system were misconfigured.
Connect today's glossary terms to this week's deliverable: what part of the artifact proves that you understand the mechanism and not just the label?
Rebuild the week from memory in under three minutes and note which transition between concepts is still weak.
quick_recap: This review day consolidates CIA Triad, Risk, and Threat into one connected model.
